questions.prompt: Which of the following should be INCLUDED as incremental cash flows in a capital-budgeting NPV?
choices: Opportunity cost of using a building you already own (instead of renting it)
Sunk cost: $2M of market research done before the decision
Cannibalization effect on existing product sales
Interest expense on debt used to finance the project
questions.explanation: Opportunity costs and cannibalization are real incremental effects. Sunk costs are gone regardless of the decision. Interest is excluded from FCF because it's captured in the discount rate; including it double-counts.

questions.prompt: FCF = NOPAT + D&A − ΔWC − CapEx. Why do we ADD back D&A?
choices: It's not a real expense at all
D&A is a non-cash deduction; NOPAT already subtracted it but no cash actually left the firm
To inflate cash flow for valuation purposes
questions.explanation: D&A reduces taxable income (good for taxes) but doesn't represent a cash outflow. To get to actual cash, you reverse it after the tax effect is captured in NOPAT.

questions.prompt: A fast-growing firm reports strong net income but is constantly short on cash. The most likely explanation is:
choices: Working capital investment (AR and inventory growing faster than collections)
questions.explanation: Each new dollar of revenue typically ties up ~$0.10-0.20 of working capital. A growing firm with positive accounting profit can still be cash-negative because of this — it's the classic 'profitable but bust' pattern.

questions.prompt: A project's year-T+1 FCF is projected at $50M, growing at 2% forever after year T. Cost of capital is 8%. What is the terminal value AT YEAR T (not discounted to today)? Answer in millions.

furtherReading.title: DCF Terminal Value Formula: How to Calculate Terminal Value
furtherReading.why: Walks through free cash flow construction and both the perpetuity-growth and exit-multiple terminal-value methods used in any capital-budgeting model.
